text.title: Code Review Agent Antipatterns
text.summary: Six agent failure modes in code review: hallucinated APIs, diff truncation, phantom security warnings, style noise, tone issues, and secret leakage.

antipattern.description: Agent suggests a fix calling a function or method that does not exist on the version of the library pinned in the project.
antipattern.reason: LLMs have training data from many library versions. Without version-pinned context, the agent suggests APIs that existed in older or newer versions but not the one in use. The author spends time investigating a non-existent function.

rule.statement: Include a snippet of package.json or requirements.txt in the review prompt. This grounds the agent in the actual dependency versions and reduces hallucinated API suggestions.
rule.rationale: Providing explicit version context shifts the agent from generating from training data to generating constrained by the actual dependency set. It does not eliminate hallucinations but reduces them significantly.

section.title: Diff Truncation

antipattern.description: Agent silently reviews only the head of a large diff and produces findings that ignore the tail of the PR, with no indication that truncation occurred.
antipattern.reason: PR diffs larger than the agent's context window are silently truncated. The agent produces a complete-looking review that misses the last 40% of changes. Reviewers trusting the agent output approve PRs with unreviewed code.

rule.statement: Enforce a 400-line PR size limit via CI. Fail loudly above 1500 lines. Never load a PR diff silently — if the diff exceeds token budget, the agent must report the truncation explicitly.
rule.rationale: Silent truncation is worse than no review because it provides false confidence. An explicit truncation error forces the team to split the PR or acknowledge that the tail was not reviewed.

section.reference: GitHub Actions workflow with pre-flight secret scan and agent review step.

antipattern.description: Agent flags SQL injection on parameterized queries, CSRF on internal services, or XSS on sanitized inputs — generating false-positive security findings that erode reviewer trust.
antipattern.reason: LLMs pattern-match on surface appearance rather than runtime behavior. A string that looks like a SQL fragment in a log message triggers injection warnings even when the actual query path is parameterized. After a few false positives, reviewers stop reading security findings.

rule.statement: Run Semgrep alongside the agent for security findings. Use Semgrep rules as the ground truth for deterministic security checks; use the agent only for design and correctness findings that rules cannot detect.
rule.rationale: Rule-based tools have low false-positive rates on the patterns they cover. Hybrid review (Semgrep for deterministic checks + agent for design) produces higher precision than either tool alone.

antipattern.description: Review output consists of 80% formatting and naming nitpicks from a mixed-formatter codebase, drowning out the BLOCKING correctness and design findings.
antipattern.reason: Without a pre-agent lint pass, the agent sees formatting inconsistencies across the entire diff and produces a high-volume of style comments. These comments make the review appear thorough while hiding the findings that actually matter.

rule.statement: Run the linter and formatter before the agent review step in CI. The agent reviews code, not formatting. Style findings from pre-lint code are noise.
rule.rationale: A lint-clean diff focuses the agent on design and logic. The signal-to-noise ratio of the review output is the key metric for whether authors read and act on agent comments.

antipattern.description: Sending a full PR diff to a third-party LLM API without scanning for secrets first, potentially exposing API keys, passwords, or tokens in the diff.
antipattern.reason: Developers occasionally commit secrets accidentally. Sending that diff to an external API creates a second exposure vector. The secret may appear in API logs, rate-limit tracking, or training data.

rule.statement: Run gitleaks or trufflehog as a pre-flight step before sending any diff to an external LLM. Block the review step if secrets are detected. Never log full diffs or prompts to stdout in public CI.
rule.rationale: Pre-flight secret scanning is the only reliable protection against accidentally sending credentials to external APIs. Blocking on detection (rather than just warning) prevents accidental bypass.
